title: Libertas - Freedom Tech Signals
description: Curated insights on freedom technology: Bitcoin, privacy tools, censorship resistance, and digital sovereignty.

items.title: Access Now: 2025 Saw Record Internet Shutdowns, 30 in 15 African Countries
items.summary: Access Now's #KeepItOn coalition documented 313 internet shutdowns globally in 2025, including 30 shutdowns across 15 African countries, with protests and elections as primary triggers.
items.content_text:
313 internet shutdowns recorded globally in 52 countries during 2025 - highest number ever documented
• Africa experienced 30 shutdowns across 15 countries, with not a single day in 2025 passing without at least one global shutdown
• Angola shut down internet for first time in July 2025 for 3+ hours during anti-government fuel price protests
• Protests were leading trigger (14 shutdowns in 8 countries), followed by election-related shutdowns (8 shutdowns in 4 countries)
• Tanzania led with 8 shutdowns including twice during elections; DRC cut internet in Goma during humanitarian crisis
• Uganda continued blocking Facebook for fifth consecutive year; Equatorial Guinea maintained isolation of Annobón island
• Civil society demonstrated resilience through litigation and alternative documentation methods during shutdowns
• African Commission on Human and Peoples Rights raised concerns about shutdown impacts on democratic processes

items.title: Iran Deploys Internet Blackout During Military Conflict; Starlink Terminals Targeted
items.summary: Iran shut down internet access during Operation Epic Fury military strikes, while regime reportedly targets Iranians using smuggled Starlink terminals for circumvention.
items.content_text:
Iranian authorities implemented internet shutdown as military strikes began during Operation Epic Fury
• Trump administration reportedly smuggled thousands of Starlink terminals into Iran prior to conflict
• Iranian regime targeting citizens using Starlink terminals, making satellite internet use punishable by prison
• Freedom House analysis compares to Putin's playbook of information control during Ukraine invasion
• US government VPN programs for circumvention were previously terminated under foreign aid cuts
• Current satellite internet requires trackable receivers that security services can disrupt
• Next-generation direct-to-phone satellite technology exists but remains undeployed at scale
• Pattern shows authoritarian regimes have mastered internet control despite early democratizing expectations

items.title: EFF: Google & Amazon Ignore Human Rights Risks in Israeli Military AI Contract
items.summary: EFF reports Google and Amazon continue Project Nimbus cloud/AI services to Israeli military despite internal warnings and documented human rights risks, while refusing transparency or meaningful action.
items.content_text:
Google's internal assessments warned of human rights abuse risks before Project Nimbus contract signing
• Contract provides Israeli Ministry of Defense with cloud computing, AI services, data analysis tools
• Amazon has completely ignored EFF letters; Google has promised responses for 1.5+ years without action
• Microsoft precedent: required public leak to investigate and confirm Israeli government service misuse
• Google's public claims about contract limitations contradict internal documentation and reporting
• Both companies' AI principles commit to human rights oversight and risk mitigation
• EFF argues companies have knowledge and leverage to act but are choosing willful blindness
• Pattern shows major cloud providers enabling surveillance infrastructure while avoiding accountability

items.title: Citizen Lab Warns of Digital Transnational Repression 'Tsunami' Facing Canadians
items.summary: Citizen Lab director testified to Parliament that digital transnational repression will intensify due to U.S. authoritarianism, AI expansion, and Canada's failure to regulate mercenary spyware as promised.
items.content_text:
Ron Deibert testified to House of Commons subcommittee on March 23 about transnational repression threats
• Digital transnational repression uses surveillance tech to harass and intimidate victims across borders
• Creates 'major chilling effects' through paranoia, isolation, and self-censorship in civil society
• Three factors accelerating threat: U.S. authoritarianism, rapid AI spread, Canada's realist foreign policy shift
• Canada pledged to regulate mercenary spyware industry but has failed to follow through
• Recommends deep engagement with affected diaspora communities to tailor responses
• Calls for review of Canada-US Safe Third Country Agreement that blocks refugee claims

items.title: US Bans All Foreign-Made Consumer Routers, Requires FCC Approval
items.summary: US now requires FCC approval for all foreign-made routers, citing supply chain vulnerabilities and cybersecurity risks. Companies must disclose foreign investors and plan US manufacturing to get approval.
items.content_text:
Executive Branch banned import/sale of new foreign-made consumer routers without FCC approval
• Policy cites supply chain vulnerabilities and cybersecurity risks to critical infrastructure
• Router manufacturers must disclose foreign investors and create US manufacturing plans
• DOD and DHS can exempt specific routers, but no exceptions listed yet
• Affects major brands like Netgear (US company manufacturing abroad)
• Starlink WiFi router appears to be main US-manufactured exception currently
• Policy will likely increase router costs as US manufacturing is more expensive
• Could impact freedom tech tools that depend on router functionality and customization

items.title: FCC Chair Uses License Threats to Enforce Pro-Trump Editorial Control Over Broadcasters
items.summary: FCC Chair Brendan Carr systematically threatens broadcast licenses to suppress coverage critical of Trump, targeting national stories while claiming to protect local interests.
items.content_text:
FCC Chair Brendan Carr threatens broadcast license revocations over coverage critical of Trump administration
• Targeted stations over Iranian missile strike coverage, immigration enforcement reporting, and Harris interview
• Used 'localism' rhetoric while actually policing national/international news content
• Delayed Paramount-Skydance merger approval until Trump received settlement payment from lawsuit
• Demanded transcripts from PBS/NPR over BBC documentary about January 6
• Threatened ABC over Jimmy Kimmel's commentary, using mafia-style language
• Launched 'Pledge America Campaign' demanding 'patriotic, pro-America content'
• Approved media consolidation deals with Trump-aligned conglomerates Nexstar and Sinclair
• Created chilling effect leading to 'rampant self-censorship' at targeted stations

items.title: Ugandan Journalist Disappeared After Discussing Struck-Down Surveillance Laws
items.summary: Journalist Mukose Arnold Anthony was forcibly disappeared by plainclothes security after discussing Constitutional Court ruling that nullified Computer Misuse Act sections used to target activists.
items.content_text:
Mukose Arnold Anthony of Alternative Digitalk forcibly taken by plainclothes men after radio broadcast on March 22
• Had just discussed Constitutional Court's March 17 ruling nullifying Computer Misuse Act sections targeting journalists
• Joint Anti-Terrorism Task Force reportedly involved, searched his home and seized electronic devices
• Police seized ID, passport, modem, laptop, and smartwatches from his residence
• Part of pattern: JATF accused of illegally detaining 100+ people, some tortured to death
• Alternative Digitalk previously raided in 2022 under now-annulled Computer Misuse Act provisions
• Police confirmed holding Mukose in custody, will arraign in court 'in due course'

items.title: UAE Criminalizes Citizen Documentation of Drone Strikes, Arrests 135+ People
items.summary: UAE authorities arrested 135+ people for filming/sharing footage of Iranian drone strikes, threatening 2+ years imprisonment and $55K fines for contradicting official narratives.
items.content_text:
UAE arrested 35 people for 'misleading content' and 100+ for filming drone strikes and sharing information online
• Dubai Police threatens minimum 2 years imprisonment and 200,000 dirham ($55K) fines for sharing 'unverified material'
• Attorney General made publication of strike images/videos illegal, encouraged reporting of violators
• Bellingcat analysis using satellite imagery contradicts multiple official UAE claims about 'successful interceptions'
• Authorities seeking phone access to determine if individuals filmed or shared footage of attacks
• British embassy confirmed at least 5 people charged under cybercrime laws for documenting strikes
• Vietnamese national detained for filming Fujairah port strike, cabin crew member arrested for sending airport explosion image
• UAE targeting citizen journalism during conflict to control information narrative

items.title: Congress Plans Clean FISA 702 Extension Despite Mass Surveillance Abuses
items.summary: Congress is moving toward an 18-month clean extension of FISA Section 702 mass surveillance powers without warrant requirements or other reforms, despite documented abuses against activists, journalists, and lawmakers.
items.content_text:
House Speaker Mike Johnson confirms plan for clean FISA Section 702 extension for at least 18 months
• House Judiciary Chair Jim Jordan now supports clean extension, abandoning previous reform positions
• 2024 RISAA reauthorization expanded surveillance powers while adding only nominal reforms
• Section 702 previously abused to spy on protesters, lawmakers, journalists, campaign donors, and judges
• Three reform bills (SAFE, PLEWSA, GSRA) available but Congress not considering them
• No warrant requirement being implemented despite House previously voting for it
• Clean extension means continued warrantless collection and querying of Americans' communications
• EFF warns mass surveillance program operates without adequate safeguards or oversight

items.title: Flock ALPR Cameras Used for Traffic Enforcement Despite Company Claims Otherwise
items.summary: Georgia police used Flock Safety's license plate readers to ticket a motorcyclist for holding a phone, contradicting the company's public claims that ALPRs aren't used for traffic violations.
items.content_text:
Georgia State Patrol issued traffic ticket based on Flock camera footage showing motorcyclist holding phone
• Ticket explicitly stated 'CAPTURED ON FLOCK CAMERA 31 MM 1 HOLDING PHONE IN LEFT HAND'
• Flock Safety previously claimed their ALPRs are 'not used to enforce traffic violations'
• Company now lists six traffic enforcement partners on their website, contradicting earlier statements
• Public records show speed enforcement cameras connected to Flock's ALPR network
• EFF warns this demonstrates classic surveillance mission creep pattern
• Case highlights gap between vendor marketing claims and actual deployment practices

items.title: Tails 7.6 Adds Automatic Tor Bridge Discovery for Censorship Circumvention
items.summary: Tails 7.6 introduces automatic Tor bridge configuration based on user region, making censorship circumvention easier for activists and journalists in restrictive networks.
items.content_text:
Tails 7.6 adds automatic Tor bridge discovery through 'Connect to Tor automatically' feature
• New 'Ask for a Tor bridge based on your region' option helps users in censorship-heavy areas
• Uses same technology as Tor Browser 11.5's connection assistant from July 2022
• Bridge discovery uses Tor Project's Moat API with domain fronting to disguise connections
• Replaces KeePassXC password manager with GNOME Secrets for better desktop integration
• Updates include Electrum 4.7.0, Tor Browser 15.0.8, and Thunderbird 140.8.0
• Improved firmware packages enhance support for newer hardware including Wi-Fi and graphics
• Automatic upgrades available from Tails 7.0+ with manual upgrade fallback option

items.title: FISA 702 Renewal Could Target VPN Users as 'Foreign' for Warrantless Surveillance
items.summary: Section 702 renewal debate reveals VPN users may be classified as 'foreign' targets, subjecting Americans to warrantless surveillance simply for using privacy tools.
items.content_text:
Congress debating renewal of FISA Section 702 with April deadline approaching
• VPN traffic routing through foreign servers may trigger 'foreign' classification under Section 702
• Americans using VPNs could lose constitutional protections and face warrantless surveillance
• Expanded definition of 'electronic communication service providers' forces more entities to assist surveillance
• Bipartisan Government Surveillance Reform Act proposes warrant requirements and closes data broker loophole
• Current law allows purchasing personal data from brokers instead of obtaining warrants
• Sen. Ron Wyden and others questioning whether intelligence agencies default-classify VPN traffic as foreign
• Proton warns that privacy tools paradoxically increase surveillance risk under current legal framework

items.title: 42 Media Orgs Challenge Trump's Executive Order Sanctioning Law Firms
items.summary: The Intercept's Press Freedom Defense Fund leads 42 media organizations filing amicus brief to block Trump's executive order that sanctions law firms representing his political opponents.
items.content_text:
42 media organizations filed amicus brief in D.C. Circuit Court challenging Trump executive order
• Executive order sanctions law firms for providing pro bono representation to Trump's political opponents
• Four lower courts already ruled the executive order unconstitutional
• Brief argues order restricts press organizations' access to legal counsel
• Coalition includes news outlets, advocacy groups, and First Amendment attorneys
• The Intercept's Press Freedom Defense Fund led the legal challenge
• Order specifically threatens outlets relying on pro bono or reduced-fee legal representation
• Legal team argues press requires oppositional relationship with government to function

items.title: Hungarian Journalist Faces Espionage Investigation After Pegasus Spyware Targeting
items.summary: Hungary orders espionage investigation of journalist Szabolcs Panyi following covert surveillance and Pegasus spyware targeting, escalating government crackdown on independent media.
items.content_text:
Hungarian journalist Szabolcs Panyi faces espionage investigation carrying up to 15 years in prison
• Investigation based on covertly recorded conversation between Panyi and source, raising surveillance concerns
• Panyi was previously targeted by Pegasus spyware, confirmed by 2023 European Parliament investigation
• Allegations stem from his reporting on Hungarian-Russian government ties and embarrassing communications
• Hungary controls ~80% of media through government-linked entities and economic pressure
• Pattern shows systematic use of surveillance and legal intimidation against independent journalists
• European Commission urged to monitor case under rule of law assessments and funding conditionality

items.title: Afghan Women Use Underground Online Education Networks to Resist Taliban Restrictions
items.summary: First-person account reveals how Afghan women are creating underground online education networks and digital resistance methods to circumvent Taliban's education bans since 2021.
items.content_text:
Personal testimony from Fareshtah, an Afghan law student whose education was cut short by Taliban takeover in August 2021
• Student prevented from defending thesis at gunpoint when universities banned women in December 2022
• Underground resistance includes online schools for girls, women's reading groups, and virtual courses
• Taliban has repeatedly reversed promises to allow women's education, forcing shift to covert online methods
• Author now teaches in clandestine online schools and university courses for women denied formal education
• Demonstrates pattern of digital education networks emerging under authoritarian censorship
• Shows evolution from formal to informal resistance education infrastructure over 3+ years

items.title: EFF's Cindy Cohn Releases Book on 30-Year Fight Against Digital Surveillance
items.summary: EFF Executive Director Cindy Cohn published 'Privacy's Defender,' documenting three decades of legal battles against government surveillance including the Crypto Wars and NSA dragnet programs.
items.content_text:
Cindy Cohn published 'Privacy's Defender: My Thirty-Year Fight Against Digital Surveillance' through MIT Press
• Book covers foundational privacy battles including Crypto Wars, NSA surveillance, and FBI gag orders
• Describes legal fights that shaped internet freedom, free speech, and privacy rights
• Featured in conversation with Cory Doctorow at San Francisco's City Lights Bookstore
• National book tour planned with stops in major US cities
• EFF developing special podcast series featuring key players and moments from the book
• References landmark cases including Bernstein v. DOJ, Hepting v. AT&T, and Jewel v. NSA

items.title: Access Now Releases 2025 Internet Shutdowns Report
items.summary: Access Now published their annual KeepItOn report documenting global internet shutdowns in 2025, highlighting rising government repression and citizen resistance.
items.content_text:
Access Now released their 2025 KeepItOn report on internet shutdowns
• Report title indicates 'rising repression meets global resistance'
• KeepItOn is the authoritative annual tracking of global internet shutdowns
• Access Now is a leading digital rights organization with established credibility
• Report likely documents government censorship tactics and their impact
• Title suggests increased pushback against shutdown policies
• Data will inform freedom tech builders on censorship patterns and needs

items.title: Supreme Court Limits ISP Copyright Liability, Protects General-Purpose Internet Services
items.summary: Supreme Court reversed billion-dollar verdict against Cox Communications, ruling ISPs can't be liable for customer copyright infringement unless they actively induce it or provide services tailored for infringement.
items.content_text:
Supreme Court reversed Fourth Circuit decision upholding $1B verdict against Cox Communications
• Court established contributory liability requires either active inducement or providing service tailored for infringement
• Mere knowledge that some customers may infringe is insufficient for liability
• Cox demonstrated good faith efforts including warnings, suspensions, and account terminations
• General-purpose internet connectivity has substantial lawful uses and can't be treated as contributory infringement
• Decision protects ISPs from pressure to over-police user activity or withdraw services
• Ruling prevents expansion of secondary liability that could chill innovation and smaller tech companies
• EFF's amicus brief arguments about patent law framework were adopted by the Court

items.title: EFF Welcomes Daily Show Viewers, Highlights 35-Year Fight Against Digital Surveillance
items.summary: The Electronic Frontier Foundation showcases its three-decade battle against government surveillance, from the Crypto Wars to NSA dragnet spying, while promoting Executive Director Cindy Cohn's new book.
items.content_text:
EFF promotes its mission to Daily Show audience, highlighting 35 years defending digital civil liberties
• Features Cindy Cohn's book 'Privacy's Defender' covering Crypto Wars, NSA spying revelations, and FBI gag order fights
• Founded in 1990 after Secret Service raids on Steve Jackson Games, establishing email privacy precedents
• Co-founded by Mitch Kapor (Lotus), John Perry Barlow (Grateful Dead), John Gilmore (Sun Microsystems), and Steve Wozniak (Apple)
• Won landmark Bernstein v. DOJ case establishing code as protected speech during encryption export restrictions
• Advocates 'Privacy First' approach to address online harms by targeting corporate surveillance infrastructure
• Organization supported by 30,000+ members, positioning itself as 'infrastructure of rebellion'
• Early case established that electronic mail deserves same protection as telephone calls

items.title: Alabama City Prosecutes Protester for Penis Costume at Anti-Trump Rally
items.summary: Fairhope, Alabama arrested and is prosecuting a 62-year-old protester for wearing an inflatable penis costume at a political rally, doubling down with additional charges despite viral backlash.
items.content_text:
Renea Gamble, 62, arrested at October 2025 No Kings protest in Fairhope, Alabama for wearing inflatable penis costume
• Police Cpl. Andrew Babb threw Gamble to ground after she attempted to leave, claiming costume violated 'community standards'
• Body camera footage contradicts officer's radio report of events, shows Gamble asking if detained before trying to leave
• City added disturbing peace and false name charges after initial disorderly conduct/resisting arrest
• Case part of broader censorship trend - Fairhope library lost state funding for refusing to move LGBTQ+ books
• Trial set for April 15 after multiple delays, despite expectations case would be dropped
• Mayor and city council publicly endorsed arrest, calling costume 'not acceptable' and violating 'community standards'

items.title: Apple Introduces OS-Level Age Verification in UK, Creating Identity-Based Access Infrastructure
items.summary: Apple now requires credit card or government ID verification for UK iPhone users to access certain features, moving age checks from apps into the operating system itself and creating infrastructure for broader identity-based restrictions.
items.content_text:
Apple implemented OS-level age verification for UK iPhones requiring credit card or government ID
• Change follows UK Online Safety Act pressure, though Apple wasn't required to implement at OS level
• Verification happens at device level before users reach apps, unlike bypassable website checks
• Creates durable link between user identity and device that persists across all apps
• Same infrastructure can enforce restrictions based on location, nationality, or other identity attributes
• California passed similar law requiring OS-level age collection; other US states considering comparable measures
• Identity-based access controls make circumvention tools and privacy apps easier to regulate
• Controls embedded in device are not easily bypassed and travel with the system

items.title: EFF Defeats Copyright Troll Attack on Activist Hosting Provider May First
items.summary: EFF successfully defended May First Movement Technology against baseless copyright demands from Higbee & Associates, protecting critical hosting infrastructure for social justice groups worldwide.
items.content_text:
Higbee & Associates targeted May First Movement Technology with copyright claim over photo posted by hosted human rights group
• May First provides web hosting to social justice organizations globally but didn't post the allegedly infringing content
• EFF represented May First, arguing hosting providers aren't liable for user-posted content under copyright law
• Higbee backed down after EFF explained legal protections for service providers who promptly remove content
• Case highlights how copyright trolling threatens infrastructure critical to activist organizations
• Statutory damages up to $150,000 per work enable mass demand letter campaigns targeting innocent parties
• Victory establishes precedent protecting hosting providers from liability for user content

items.title: EFF's Cindy Cohn on Daily Show to Discuss 30 Years of Privacy Advocacy
items.summary: EFF Executive Director Cindy Cohn appears on The Daily Show to discuss her new book covering three decades fighting surveillance, from the Crypto Wars to NSA dragnet spying.
items.content_text:
Cindy Cohn, EFF Executive Director, featured on The Daily Show March 30
• Promoting new book 'Privacy's Defender: My Thirty-Year Fight Against Digital Surveillance' (MIT Press)
• Book covers her role in major digital rights battles including 1990s Crypto Wars
• Details resistance to NSA's dragnet internet surveillance programs revealed in 2000s
• Describes fights against FBI gag orders and support for whistleblowers
• Represents broader EFF work defending technologists, innovators, and researchers
• Mainstream media platform amplifies decades of freedom tech advocacy work

items.title: Italian Prosecutors Confirm Journalist Hacked with Paragon 'Ethical' Spyware
items.summary: Italian prosecutors validated Citizen Lab's forensics showing journalist Francesco Cancellato was targeted by Paragon's Graphite spyware, undermining claims of 'ethical' commercial surveillance tools.
items.content_text:
Italian prosecutors officially confirmed journalist Francesco Cancellato was hacked with spyware
• Citizen Lab's forensic analysis identified Paragon's Graphite spyware as the attack vector
• WhatsApp initially alerted Cancellato to the suspected spyware infection
• Case challenges Paragon's marketing claims about 'ethical' surveillance technology use
• Prosecution validation raises questions about prior Italian investigations that found nothing
• Demonstrates continued targeting of journalists with commercial spyware tools
• Adds to growing evidence of mercenary spyware industry's threat to press freedom

items.title: Survey: 78% of US Parents Concerned About Children's Digital Privacy
items.summary: Proton survey reveals widespread parental anxiety about children's online privacy, with 58% fearing identity theft before age 18 and concerns about data collection by apps and schools.
items.content_text:
78% of US parents concerned about child's online privacy, with 56% 'very concerned'
• 58% of parents worry about identity theft occurring before child reaches 18
• 76% of children already own smartphones/tablets, with age 10 being biggest jump in device ownership
• 44% of children have email addresses, 42% have social media accounts
• Top platforms used by children: TikTok (63%), Facebook (53%), Instagram (50%), Snapchat (45%)
• 65% of parents worried educational platforms collect excessive data about children
• One-third of parents discovered apps collected more child data than expected in past year
• Parents fear digital footprints could affect future employment (55%), education (56%), and safety (70%)

items.title: UK Advances Social Media Ban with Ministerial Powers to Restrict Internet Access for Under-18s
items.summary: UK Parliament is progressing legislation that would give ministers sweeping powers to ban social media for under-18s and restrict VPN use without accountability mechanisms or evidence requirements.
items.content_text:
House of Commons defeated Lords' amendment to ban under-16s from social media by 307 to 173 votes
• Commons proposed alternative giving Secretary of State power to restrict internet access for under-18s
• New powers include ability to limit VPN use and restrict 'addictive features' without evidence requirements
• Authority shifts from independent regulator Ofcom to political ministers with no accountability mechanisms
• Broad definitions could target LGBTQ+ content and sex education behind age gates
• 107 Labour MPs abstained, showing party split on the issue
• Amendment modifies existing Online Safety Act enforcement framework

items.title: Nigerian Press Freedom Groups Document 231+ Journalist Attacks Under Tinubu Administration
items.summary: Press freedom organizations formally challenge VP Shettima's claim of no journalist harassment in Nigeria, documenting 231 verified attacks since 2023 including detentions, assaults, and killings.
items.content_text:
Joint letter from 6 major press freedom organizations to President Tinubu disputes VP's claims
• Centre for Journalism Innovation documented 231 verified attacks on press since Tinubu took office in 2023
• At least 56 journalists assaulted during #EndBadGovernance protests, 18 had bullets/tear gas fired at them
• Onifade Emmanuel Pelumi killed in police custody 2020, body still not returned to family after 5 years
• Pattern of impunity: CPJ documented 23 journalist killings in Nigeria since 1992, 13 work-related
• Organizations warn of expected spike in attacks during 2027 election period
• Government sought to appeal court judgment ordering improved press freedom protections
• Signatories include CPJ, Media Rights Agenda, International Press Centre, and other established groups

items.title: Russia Labels Press Freedom Org IWMF as 'Undesirable Organization'
items.summary: Russia designated the International Women's Media Foundation as an 'undesirable organization,' banning its operations and criminalizing support with up to 6 years prison.
items.content_text:
Russia labeled International Women's Media Foundation (IWMF) as 'undesirable organization' in January 2026
• Designation bans IWMF from operating in Russia and criminalizes participation with up to 6 years prison
• Distributing IWMF content or donating to the organization now carries administrative and criminal penalties
• IWMF was not informed of reasons behind the classification [Source]
• Committee to Protect Journalists and Journalist Assistance Network condemned the move
• JAN includes CPJ, IWMF, Freedom of the Press Foundation, PEN America, and Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press
• Tactic aims to sever support networks that sustain independent journalists under threat
• Pattern matches previous Russian use of 'undesirable' classification against press freedom organizations

items.title: US Rewrites Procurement Rules to Force AI Companies to Disable Safety Guardrails
items.summary: New GSA procurement rules would require AI contractors to license systems for 'all lawful purposes' and disable safety guardrails that refuse government requests, effectively weaponizing procurement against AI safety measures.
items.content_text:
General Services Administration (GSA) proposing new federal procurement rules for AI systems
• Rules would require contractors to license AI tools for 'all lawful purposes' to government
• Companies must disable safety guardrails that might refuse government data requests
• Move follows public dispute between Department of Defense and Anthropic over surveillance use
• EFF and partners filed joint comments opposing the rules as threat to AI safety
• Provisions would become standard in all federal contracts if adopted
• Government using procurement power to override company resistance to surveillance applications
• Rules include 'anti-woke' requirements that are 'technologically incoherent' [EFF]

items.title: EFF Promotes New Book on 30 Years of Digital Surveillance Resistance
items.summary: Electronic Frontier Foundation's Executive Director Cindy Cohn published 'Privacy's Defender,' chronicling three decades of legal battles against government surveillance from the Crypto Wars to NSA dragnet spying.
items.content_text:
EFF Executive Director Cindy Cohn published 'Privacy's Defender: My Thirty-Year Fight Against Digital Surveillance' (MIT Press)
• Book covers legal battles from 1990s Crypto Wars, NSA internet spying revelations, and FBI gag order resistance
• EFF founded in 1990 after Steve Jackson Games case established email deserves same protection as telephone calls
• Organization has 30,000+ members and 35+ years defending digital civil liberties
• EFF promotes 'Privacy First' approach to addressing online harms through limiting corporate surveillance
• Bernstein v. DOJ case mentioned as precedent-setting encryption publication rights case
• Content serves as recruitment tool following Daily Show interview appearance

items.title: The Intercept Guide: Protecting Devices from ICE/CBP Searches at US Airports
items.summary: Comprehensive security guide for protecting digital devices from Immigration and Customs Enforcement and border agents at US airports, covering throwaway devices, encryption, and operational security practices.
items.content_text:
ICE agents now deployed to dozens of US airports with expanded device search authority
• CBP can examine traveler devices without warrant, recent cases include Norwegian tourist denied entry over phone content
• Best practice: Use throwaway travel devices with clean accounts instead of personal devices
• Disable biometrics, use strong alphanumeric passcodes, power devices completely off at checkpoints
• Enable password manager travel modes and delete sensitive apps/contacts before travel
• Travelers can refuse to unlock devices but face delays, harassment, and indefinite confiscation
• Use tools like Cryptomator to encrypt sensitive files in cloud storage, delete local copies
• Defense-in-depth approach assumes device compromise and minimizes stored sensitive data

items.title: EFF Director Cindy Cohn Discusses Privacy Book on The Daily Show
items.summary: EFF Executive Director Cindy Cohn appeared on The Daily Show to discuss her new book covering 30 years of digital privacy battles including the Crypto Wars and NSA surveillance.
items.content_text:
Cindy Cohn appeared on The Daily Show with Jon Stewart on March 30
• Promoted her book 'Privacy's Defender: My Thirty-Year Fight Against Digital Surveillance'
• Book covers her role in the Crypto Wars of the 1990s
• Details battles against NSA's dragnet internet spying programs revealed in 2000s
• Discusses fights against FBI gag orders and digital surveillance overreach
• Represents EFF's advocacy for technology users, innovators, and whistleblowers
• Major media platform exposure for digital rights and privacy issues

items.title: EFF Analyzes Digital Legacy of Arab Spring: From Liberation Tool to Repression Infrastructure
items.summary: EFF launches series examining how 2011 Arab uprisings transformed digital resistance tactics and government countermeasures, showing how liberation tools became surveillance infrastructure.
items.content_text:
EFF begins series analyzing 15-year digital legacy of 2011 Arab Spring uprisings
• Documents how same tools used for organizing became instruments for tracking and prosecuting dissidents
• Tunisia had sophisticated filtering regime: DNS tampering, URL blocking, IP filtering, keyword censorship
• Tunisian bloggers built resilient culture using circumvention tools to push information beyond borders
• Egypt's government blocked Twitter/Facebook, then implemented near-total internet shutdown
• Governments learned from each other, developing playbook to disrupt networks and identify organizers
• Internet blackouts evolved from rare occurrence to normalized crisis response tool
• Series will examine how states consolidated online control and platforms narrowed dissent space

items.title: EFF Promotes Digital Rights Advocacy and Privacy Education to Daily Show Viewers
items.summary: EFF's outreach page highlights 35 years of defending digital rights, from crypto wars to NSA surveillance battles, showcasing their role as infrastructure defenders for online rebellion.
items.content_text:
EFF targets Daily Show viewers with educational content about digital rights work
• Organization has 35-year history fighting government surveillance and corporate overreach
• Key victories include Bernstein v. DOJ establishing code as protected speech
• Successfully challenged NSA dragnet surveillance and FBI gag orders
• Current focus includes AI regulation, corporate surveillance, and police technology tracking
• Develops free privacy tools and maintains surveillance documentation projects
• Executive Director Cindy Cohn published memoir on three-decade anti-surveillance fight
• Founded in 1990 after government raids on Steve Jackson Games highlighted need for tech-savvy civil liberties defense

items.title: EFF Challenges FCC Chair's Unconstitutional Threats Against Broadcasters
items.summary: EFF and civil liberties groups condemn FCC Chair Brendan Carr's threats to revoke broadcast licenses for unfavorable coverage of Trump, calling them unconstitutional censorship attempts.
items.content_text:
EFF joined coalition opposing FCC Chair Brendan Carr's threats against broadcasters
• Carr threatened license revocation for stations airing content critical of Trump administration
• Civil liberties groups argue threats violate First Amendment protections
• Carr claims 'public interest' standard justifies punishing unfavorable news coverage
• Coalition argues 'public interest' has never permitted viewpoint-based punishment
• Carr's 'falsity' allegations target policy disagreements and criticism, not accuracy
• Groups specifically cite Iran War coverage as example of prohibited government line enforcement
• Coalition demands Carr withdraw unconstitutional threats

items.title: Vietnamese Journalist Gets 8.5 Years for Social Media Posts Critical of State
items.summary: Vietnam sentenced journalist Huynh Ngoc Tuan to 8.5 years in prison for Facebook posts and videos deemed anti-state propaganda, highlighting the need for censorship-resistant publishing tools.
items.content_text:
Vietnamese court sentenced journalist Huynh Ngoc Tuan to 8 years, 6 months under anti-propaganda laws
• Conviction based on 11 live videos and 21 Facebook posts about politics and human rights
• Tuan held incommunicado for 6 months without lawyer access before closed one-day trial
• Family subjected to ongoing harassment, surveillance, and equipment confiscation
• Tuan previously served 10 years for critical writings; daughter served nearly 3 years for blogging
• Vietnam currently imprisons 17 journalists, among world's worst for press freedom
• Case demonstrates vulnerability of centralized platforms to state censorship and surveillance

items.title: Citizen Lab Submits Digital Transnational Repression Framework to UN
items.summary: Citizen Lab submitted recommendations to the UN on digital transnational repression - governments using digital tools to surveil and silence human rights defenders across borders.
items.content_text:
Citizen Lab submitted formal input to UN High Commissioner on Human Rights on digital transnational repression
• Digital transnational repression defined as governments using digital tools to surveil, intimidate, and silence diaspora activists
• Submission calls for UN to identify, document, and characterize these acts as human rights violations
• Recommendations target three actors: UN bodies, host countries, and technology companies
• Built on extensive Citizen Lab research documenting repression methods and victim impacts
• Aims to establish formal UN framework for addressing cross-border digital surveillance
• Could influence international policy on state surveillance of diaspora communities

items.title: Japan's Team Mirai Uses Open-Source AI Tools to Reshape Democratic Participation
items.summary: Japan's Team Mirai party won 11 seats using open-source AI tools for direct voter engagement, policy formation, and transparency - demonstrating scalable digital democracy infrastructure.
items.content_text:
Team Mirai won nearly 4 million votes and 11 seats in Japan's House of Representatives using AI-driven voter engagement
• Party's AI Interviewer facilitated 8,000+ hours of direct voter policy input since 2025
• Action Board app captured 100,000 organizer actions daily during election runup
• All software tools (Gikai assembly app, Mirumae transparency tool) are open-source and freely available
• Party formed policy platform from 38,000+ online questions and 6,000+ policy suggestions via AI Policy app
• Secured agreement with Japan's ruling LDP party to adopt Team Mirai's transparency tools
• Founded by software engineer Takahiro Anno, party positions itself as 'utility infrastructure' for democracy
• Demonstrates technology enhancing rather than undermining democratic processes through direct participation

items.title: FISA 702 Renewal Could Strip VPN Users of Constitutional Protections
items.summary: US surveillance law renewal may classify VPN traffic as 'foreign' by default, potentially subjecting American VPN users to warrantless surveillance under Section 702.
items.content_text:
Section 702 of FISA allows warrantless surveillance of 'foreign' communications, routinely capturing Americans' data
• VPN traffic routing through foreign servers may be classified as 'foreign' under current surveillance rules
• Classification could strip VPN users of constitutional protections and place them in Section 702 pipeline
• Expanded definition of 'electronic communication service providers' now includes cloud services, WiFi networks
• Senators Ron Wyden and Mike Lee propose Government Surveillance Reform Act requiring warrants for Americans' data
• Bill would close loophole allowing government to buy personal data from brokers without court oversight
• Bipartisan opposition includes protests at House Intelligence Committee Democrat Jim Himes' town halls
• April deadline approaching for Section 702 renewal with minimal proposed changes

items.title: Ugandan Journalist Disappeared After Discussing Struck-Down Digital Censorship Laws
items.summary: Arnold Anthony Mukose, journalist and program director at Alternative Digitalk, was forcibly seized by security operatives after broadcasting about a court ruling that nullified Uganda's Computer Misuse Act.
items.content_text:
Arnold Anthony Mukose forcibly taken by plainclothes security officers outside Metro FM on March 22, 2026
• Seizure occurred after live broadcast discussing Constitutional Court ruling that struck down Computer Misuse Act sections
• Joint Anti-Terrorism Task Force conducted house search, seizing electronic devices including laptop, modem, and smart watches
• Computer Misuse Act had been frequently used to target journalists and digital rights activists
• Police confirmed custody but timeline for court appearance unclear
• Pattern matches Uganda's documented history of enforced disappearances targeting media and opposition
• Alternative Digitalk previously raided in 2022, staff charged under now-annulled Computer Misuse Act provisions

items.title: Proton Meet Launches with MLS Encryption to Counter AI Video Surveillance
items.summary: Proton launched Meet, an end-to-end encrypted video conferencing service using MLS protocol, addressing AI-powered surveillance risks in mainstream platforms like Zoom and Google Meet.
items.content_text:
Proton Meet launched as encrypted alternative to Zoom, Google Meet, and Microsoft Teams
• Uses Messaging Layer Security (MLS) protocol for end-to-end encryption at enterprise scale
• Built on WebRTC with Selective Forwarding Units (SFU) for scalability without P2P IP exposure
• Addresses AI surveillance threat from Big Tech companies recording/analyzing meeting data
• Features zero-knowledge meeting authentication via SRP - Proton cannot access meetings
• Offers web browser access without account required, plus dedicated mobile/desktop apps
• Free tier available with enterprise-grade features and admin controls
• Distributed global server network for low-latency, high-reliability communications

items.title: NYC Ends $4M Palantir Hospital Contract After Activist Pressure
items.summary: New York City's public hospital system will not renew its $4 million Palantir contract in October after sustained activist pressure against the surveillance company's presence in healthcare.
items.content_text:
NYC Health + Hospitals paid Palantir ~$4 million since 2023 for data analysis services including automated scanning of patient health notes
• Contract enabled Palantir to help with Medicaid billing efficiency through patient data analysis
• Activists protested the deal citing Palantir's role in lethal airstrikes, mass surveillance, and ICE deportation raids
• CEO Mitchell Katz announced at March 16 city council meeting that contract expires in October and won't be renewed
• Hospital system claims 'absolute firewall' prevented patient data sharing with Palantir's government clients like ICE
• Data analysis functions will be brought in-house after contract expiration
• American Friends Service Committee led campaign that successfully pressured the hospital system
• Activists vow to continue targeting Palantir contracts with other institutions globally

items.title: EFF Warns UN: Cybercrime Laws and Surveillance Targeting Human Rights Defenders
items.summary: EFF submission to UN documents how cybercrime laws, digital surveillance, and platform policies are being weaponized against human rights defenders globally, with insufficient safeguards.
items.content_text:
EFF submitted analysis to UN Office of High Commissioner for Human Rights on threats to digital rights defenders
• Cybercrime and national security laws increasingly used to prosecute lawful expression and expand surveillance
• UK's Online Safety Act spreading 'duty of care' frameworks globally with weaker protections in some jurisdictions
• Internet shutdowns, throttling, and geo-blocking disrupting HRDs' ability to communicate and document abuses
• Cross-border spyware and biometric surveillance deployed against activists and journalists
• Platform content moderation systems removing documentation of human rights violations through automated enforcement
• EFF calls for narrowly tailored legal frameworks, independent oversight, and meaningful civil society engagement

items.title: EFF: States Push DRM-Style 'Print Blocking' Requirements for 3D Printers
items.summary: Three U.S. states propose mandating 3D printer manufacturers install algorithmic censorship to block 'gun-like' shapes, with criminal penalties for bypassing the restrictions.
items.content_text:
Three U.S. states proposed laws requiring 3D printer manufacturers to install 'print blocking' algorithms
• Proposed systems would scan prints for 'forbidden shapes' deemed too gun-like by vendors
• Criminal penalties proposed for bypassing restrictions or reselling unrestricted printers
• EFF argues this creates DRM-style vendor lock-in similar to other anti-competitive practices
• Laws would restrict printers to manufacturer-only software, blocking open source alternatives
• Bambu Labs recently forced restrictive firmware updates limiting OrcaSlicer compatibility
• Targeted 'ghost gun' production already illegal in proposing states
• Technical implementation faces cat-and-mouse detection problems covered in EFF's part 2

items.title: EFF's 2026 Foilies Expose Government Transparency Failures
items.summary: EFF and MuckRock's annual "Foilies" highlight worst government transparency violations, including Texas hiding Abbott-Musk communications and DHS abandoning text message archiving.
items.content_text:
EFF and MuckRock released 2026 Foilies recognizing worst government transparency failures during Sunshine Week
• Texas Gov. Greg Abbott's office released 1,400 pages of Abbott-Musk communications with 1,200 pages fully redacted
• Department of Homeland Security stopped automatically archiving official text messages, now relies on manual screenshots
• Vancouver, B.C. imposed $10 CAD fee for non-personal public records requests despite tax funding
• FCC delayed student FOIA requests for 7+ months after previously responding within days/weeks
• Awards highlight systematic erosion of transparency rights across multiple jurisdictions
• Positive example: Northern Nevada Public Health delivers restaurant inspection records within 20 minutes
• Federal Records Act requires agencies to retain all official communications, making DHS policy potentially illegal

items.title: 2025 Broke Records: 313 Internet Shutdowns Across 52 Countries, Zero Days Without Blackouts
items.summary: Access Now reports 2025 had 313 internet shutdowns in 52 countries with no single day free of blackouts. Latin America saw Venezuela continue pattern plus Panama's first shutdowns during social security protests.
items.content_text:
2025 recorded 313 internet shutdowns across 52 countries - a new global record [Access Now]
• Every single day of 2025 had at least one internet shutdown occurring somewhere globally
• Latin America/Caribbean: 5 shutdowns in 2 countries - Venezuela (repeat offender) and Panama (first-time)
• Panama blocked internet/mobile in Bocas del Toro province for 2 weeks during social security reform protests
• 40% of shutdowns (125) were conflict-related across 14 countries
• 70 shutdowns coincided with serious human rights violations including killings, torture, war crimes
• 100 countries total have experienced connectivity shutdowns since Access Now monitoring began in 2016
• Shutdowns used to influence elections, silence populations, and enable rights violations with impunity

items.title: US Government Proposes Rules Forcing AI Companies to Disable Safety Features
items.summary: General Services Administration proposes procurement rules requiring contractors to license AI systems for 'all lawful purposes' and disable safety guardrails that might refuse government requests.
items.content_text:
GSA proposes new federal procurement rules targeting AI contractors
• Rules would require companies to license AI systems for 'all lawful purposes' to government
• Companies must disable safety guardrails that might refuse government data requests
• Follows ongoing dispute between Department of Defense and Anthropic over surveillance use
• EFF, CDT, Protect Democracy Project, and EPIC filed joint opposition comments
• Rules would become standard components of every federal contract if adopted
• Government using procurement power to bypass companies' refusal to enable mass surveillance
• Proposal includes 'technologically incoherent anti-Woke requirements' [Source]

items.title: Major Publishers Block Internet Archive Over AI Concerns, Threatening Historical Web Record
items.summary: The New York Times and other publishers are blocking Internet Archive crawlers due to AI scraping fears, potentially erasing decades of preserved web history that researchers and journalists depend on.
items.content_text:
New York Times began blocking Internet Archive crawlers using technical measures beyond robots.txt
• The Guardian and other major publishers reportedly following similar blocking practices
• Publishers cite concerns about AI companies scraping news content as primary motivation
• Internet Archive preserves over 1 trillion web pages used daily by journalists, researchers, and courts
• Wikipedia alone links to 2.6 million news articles preserved at the Archive across 249 languages
• EFF argues archiving qualifies as fair use, similar to search engine protections
• Blocking threatens historical record that often shows original versions of edited or removed articles
• Risk of irreversible loss of three decades of preserved web content

items.title: EFF Analysis: State-Mandated 3D Printer Censorship Will Fail but Harm Users
items.summary: EFF explains why proposed state laws requiring "print blockers" on 3D printers won't prevent firearm manufacturing but will create censorship infrastructure that harms legitimate users and manufacturers.
items.content_text:
Multiple US states proposing laws to mandate "print blocking" technology on 3D printers and CNC machines
• Laws would require printers to scan files against government-maintained database of prohibited designs
• EFF argues technical implementation is fundamentally flawed - printers use G-Code instructions, not 3D models
• Proposed restrictions would lock users into vendor software and create ongoing compliance costs
• Many printer computers lack computational power to render G-Code back into 3D models for comparison
• Laws would criminalize circumventing scanning or loading alternative software on owned devices
• Existing noncompliant printers could not be legally resold in regulated states
• Technology creates foundation for broader 3D-print censorship platform in other jurisdictions

items.title: EFF Urges Supreme Court to Hold Cisco Liable for Torture-Enabling Surveillance System
items.summary: EFF filed Supreme Court brief arguing Cisco should face liability for custom-building China's 'Golden Shield' surveillance system used to persecute, torture Falun Gong practitioners.
items.content_text:
EFF filed amicus brief in Cisco Systems v. Doe I case before U.S. Supreme Court
• Cisco custom-built 'Golden Shield' surveillance system for Chinese government targeting Falun Gong
• System enabled tracking, detention, and torture - intercepted communications used during torture sessions
• 9th Circuit ruled U.S. companies can be liable under Alien Tort Statute for aiding human rights abuses
• Court held companies need only 'knowledge' not 'purpose' that assistance enables abuse
• Supreme Court oral arguments scheduled for April 28, 2025
• Case could set precedent for accountability of surveillance tech companies globally
• EFF argues this goes beyond general-purpose tech to purposeful persecution assistance

items.title: Hungary Files Espionage Charges Against Journalist Who Exposed Russian Influence Operations
items.summary: Hungarian government charged investigative journalist Szabolcs Panyi with espionage after he reported on Russian influence operations, escalating press freedom attacks ahead of elections.
items.content_text:
Hungary filed espionage charges against Direkt36/VSquare journalist Szabolcs Panyi on March 26, 2026
• Charges followed Panyi's reporting on Russian influence operations ahead of April 12 parliamentary elections
• Government alleges Panyi spied 'in coordination with a foreign country' and intercepted communications between Hungarian Foreign Minister and Russian officials
• Panyi was previously surveilled with Pegasus spyware in 2021, indicating sustained government targeting
• Espionage conviction carries 8-15 year prison sentence depending on classification level
• State Sovereignty Protection Office labeled Panyi as 'financed and directed by European Commission and U.S. Democratic-party linked backers'
• Committee to Protect Journalists calls tactics 'straight out of Putin's playbook' and unprecedented attack on EU press freedom
• Pattern demonstrates escalating authoritarian tactics to silence independent media before elections

items.title: EFF Warns Against 3D Printer DRM Laws in Three US States
items.summary: Three US states propose laws requiring 3D printer manufacturers to install 'print-blocking' software to detect gun-like shapes, effectively mandating DRM that would restrict open-source software and enable vendor surveillance.
items.content_text:
Three US states proposed laws mandating 3D printers include algorithmic 'print-blocking' to detect forbidden shapes
• Bills would require printers only work with manufacturer software, criminalizing bypasses or reselling unrestricted devices
• EFF argues this creates DRM system that punishes law-abiding users while being easily bypassed by criminals
• Would eliminate open-source alternatives like OrcaSlicer, forcing users into vendor lock-in arrangements
• Print-blocking targets 'ghost guns' but these are typically mass-produced kits, not home-printed items
• Criminal penalties proposed for anyone bypassing censorship software or circumventing restrictions
• Pattern mirrors historical DRM implementation that enables anti-competitive manufacturer control
• Threatens 3D printing's role as democratized manufacturing tool for innovation and research

items.title: Expert Warns of Privatized Offensive Cyber Operations Under Trump Admin
items.summary: Citizen Lab director warns Trump administration may authorize private firms to conduct offensive cyber operations, creating unprecedented risks for oversight, civil liberties, and global cyber security.
items.content_text:
Citizen Lab director Ron Deibert published warning in Lawfare about potential policy shift
• Trump administration may authorize private companies to conduct offensive cyber operations (OCOs)
• OCOs aim to disrupt and destroy adversary systems, unlike espionage which gathers information
• Would represent unprecedented departure from U.S. military convention and international norms
• Risks include compromised oversight, counterintelligence vulnerabilities, and empowering corrupt industry
• Could accelerate global cyber arms race and increase risks for civilians and states
• Deibert recommends international coalition against these developments
• Calls for increased resources for civil society watchdog organizations

items.title: EFF Analysis: How Governments Evolved From Crude Blocking to Sophisticated Censorship Systems
items.summary: EFF documents how government censorship evolved from simple website blocks that VPNs could bypass to sophisticated platform control systems, using Egypt's transformation from Arab Spring catalyst to digital dystopia as a case study.
items.content_text:
66% of internet users now live where political/social sites are blocked, up from crude blocking 15 years ago
• 78% are in countries where people face arrest for online posts, with new social media regulations in dozens of countries
• Egypt went from Facebook-sparked revolution in 2011 to imprisoning digital rights defenders under broad cybercrime laws
• 2018 Egyptian Cybercrime Law allows website blocking for vague 'national security' threats, criminalizes accessing banned sites
• Articles 25-26 used to prosecute social media users for 'violating family values' - many targets are young women
• Governments shifted from reactive blocking to proactive platform pressure, making VPN circumvention less effective
• Early 2000s government responses (Thailand blocking YouTube, Turkey takedown demands) were testing grounds for current systems

items.title: Afghan Journalist's Letters Spark Cross-Border Solidarity in China Despite Censorship
items.summary: Afghan journalist Khadija Haidary's correspondence about Taliban oppression, translated and shared on Chinese social media, generated grassroots support that helped fund her family's resettlement in Canada.
items.content_text:
Afghan journalist Khadija Haidary's email correspondence about life under Taliban was translated by Chinese journalist Weilin Hong in October 2024
• Letters published on WeChat account 'Positive Links' went viral among Chinese readers despite tight media controls
• Chinese publisher offered book contract and advance royalties, enabling Haidary's family to resettle from Pakistan to Canada
• Over 10,000 copies sold by August 2025, with readers promoting book through reviews on Xiaohongshu, Weibo, and other platforms
• Cross-border solidarity emerged through individual acts rather than organized movement due to China's constrained civil society
• Chinese readers drew parallels between Afghan women's experiences and their own restrictions on feminist expression
• Support network utilized multiple platforms (WeChat, Telegram, Douban) to circumvent information controls
• Demonstrates how personal narratives can bypass official media filters and generate meaningful activism under authoritarian constraints

items.title: Sen. Wyden Warns of Classified Section 702 Surveillance Abuse
items.summary: Sen. Ron Wyden revealed existence of classified Section 702 surveillance abuse that affects Americans' privacy rights, warning Congress is debating reauthorization without full information.
items.content_text:
Sen. Wyden disclosed existence of classified Section 702 surveillance abuse during Senate floor speech
• Abuse 'directly affects the privacy rights of Americans' according to Wyden
• Multiple administrations have refused declassification requests for years
• Wyden warns Congress is debating Section 702 reauthorization 'with insufficient information'
• Speech delivered during protest of Joshua Rudd's NSA nomination over constitutional concerns
• Wyden predicts Americans will be 'stunned' when abuse is eventually declassified
• Section 702 reauthorization deadline approaching, creating legislative pressure point

items.title: Iran: Thousands of Prisoners at Risk Amid Escalating Conflict and Mass Executions
items.summary: Human Rights Watch reports thousands of Iranian detainees, including political prisoners and activists, face dual threats from US/Israeli strikes and mass executions by authorities who continue arresting dissidents for documenting attacks.
items.content_text:
Thousands of prisoners in Iran at risk from military strikes and state-executed atrocities [Human Rights Watch]
• Iranian authorities conducting mass arbitrary detentions of protesters, activists, and dissidents since February 2026
• 446 people arrested for 'disturbing public opinion' and sharing footage/photos of strikes with media
• At least 8 men arbitrarily executed on political charges between March 18-31, 2026
• Prisoners detained in facilities targeted by US/Israeli strikes, including police stations and IRGC facilities
• Authorities threatening protesters with lethal force, warning they will be treated as 'enemies'
• Prison conditions deteriorating with reduced food, denied medical care, and violent suppression of protests
• Iran has legal mechanisms for humanitarian prisoner release during crises but refuses to implement them

items.title: Internet Shutdowns Hit Record High: 313 Documented in 2025 Across 52 Countries
items.summary: Access Now documents 313 internet shutdowns in 52 countries during 2025, increasingly used to conceal war crimes and human rights abuses, with growing attacks on satellite internet systems.
items.content_text:
313 internet shutdowns documented in 52 countries in 2025, surpassing 2024's record of 304
• Seven new countries joined offender list, bringing total to 100 countries affected since 2016
• Conflict remains leading trigger with 125 shutdowns across 14 countries to conceal war crimes
• Sharp increase in targeting Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite systems: 14 shutdowns vs 4 in 2024
• 70 shutdowns coincided with grave human rights abuses including murder, torture, and war crimes
• Myanmar leads with 95 shutdowns, followed by India (65), Pakistan (20), and Iran (11)
• Russia imposed hundreds to thousands of disruptions, officially justified as drone countermeasures
• 75 shutdowns persisted from 2025 into 2026, indicating trend toward permanent communications blocks
• Growing resistance: ICC recognizing shutdowns as linked to crimes against humanity, election observers condemning practice

items.title: CBP Orders $50M Arsenal of Chemical Weapons for Crowd Control
items.summary: U.S. Customs and Border Protection plans to purchase up to $50 million in tear gas, projectiles, and other 'less lethal' weapons, including 123 different munition types for potential use against protesters.
items.content_text:
CBP procurement documents reveal plans to buy 123 types of 'less lethal' munitions across 10 categories
• Arsenal includes CS tear gas (banned for military use) and OC pepper spray in various delivery systems
• Purchase quantities include 242,000+ pyrotechnic canisters and 100,000+ impact munition rounds
• Weapons include 'ferret rounds' designed to penetrate barriers and 175-decibel sound grenades
• Medical experts warn of reproductive health impacts from chemical exposure and permanent hearing damage
• Procurement follows documented abuse of similar weapons against protesters during previous administration
• Rubber-ball grenades and scattershot projectiles mirror weapons used by Chilean and Iranian forces against demonstrators

items.title: Record 313 Internet Shutdowns in 2025, Eastern Europe/Central Asia Hit 29 Times
items.summary: Access Now reports 2025 saw the highest number of internet shutdowns ever recorded globally (313 across 52 countries), with Eastern Europe and Central Asia experiencing 29 shutdowns across 7 countries.
items.content_text:
313 internet shutdowns documented globally in 2025, affecting 52 countries with no shutdown-free days
• Eastern Europe and Central Asia saw 29 shutdowns across Belarus, Kyrgyzstan, Russia, Turkmenistan, and Ukraine
• Russia led regional shutdowns, often justified as drone attack countermeasures with limited evidence of necessity
• Belarus imposed platform blocks and throttling around January 2025 presidential election
• Civil society fighting back through strategic litigation in Kazakhstan challenging shutdown legality
• Hundreds to thousands of additional disruptions reported across Russia since May 2025, still being verified
• Shutdowns increasingly normalized as tools of political control during elections and security tensions
• Pattern spreading across region, risking normalization of shutdowns as default government response

items.title: FAA Issues Nationwide 21-Month Drone Ban Near ICE Vehicles, EFF Challenges
items.summary: Trump administration issued unprecedented nationwide flight restriction banning drones within half-mile of ICE/CBP vehicles for 21 months, effectively criminalizing accountability journalism.
items.content_text:
FAA issued TFR FDC 6/4375 on January 16, 2026, lasting until October 29, 2027 - 21 months total
• Restriction covers entire US, bans drones within 3000 feet of any DHS/DOJ/DOD/DOE vehicles including ICE/CBP
• Violators face criminal charges, civil penalties, and drone seizure/destruction
• EFF and major news organizations demanded FAA lift restriction in January, no response after 2+ months
• ICE often uses unmarked rental cars and swaps license plates, making compliance impossible
• Timing coincides with Minneapolis anti-ICE protests and killings of Renée Good and Alex Pretti
• EFF argues violation of First Amendment right to record police and Fifth Amendment due process
• FAA failed to specify hazard justification or provide media contact as required by own regulations

items.title: Bangladesh's 2026 Election: 72 Cases of AI-Generated Disinformation Documented
items.summary: Study identifies 72 cases of AI-manipulated content during Bangladesh's February 2026 election, including deepfakes of political figures and fabricated media photocards designed to shape electoral outcomes.
items.content_text:
First comprehensive study of AI disinformation in Bangladesh's post-uprising election identified 72 manipulation cases
• 49% of AI content created entirely fictional realities about political leaders' activities and statements
• 28% involved fake statement attributions using AI-edited photocards mimicking trusted media outlets
• Deepfakes targeted all major parties: fabricated videos of exiled PM Sheikh Hasina and fake endorsement from Malaysian PM Mahathir
• AI-generated images showed new PM Tarique Rahman at events he never attended
• Synthetic content exploited shooting of activist Osman Hadi, spreading false recovery images
• Fabricated photocards from established outlets like Somoy TV and Channel i amplified false quotes
• Study demonstrates systematic weaponization of AI across multiple political factions

items.title: US iPhone Hacking Toolkit 'Coruna' Leaked to Russia, Now Used by Adversaries
items.summary: Google researchers exposed 'Coruna,' a sophisticated US government iPhone hacking toolkit that exploits 23 iOS vulnerabilities, reportedly leaked by L3Harris employee to Russia and now used by criminal groups.
items.content_text:
Google Project Zero identified 'Coruna,' an advanced iPhone exploitation toolkit using 23 iOS vulnerabilities
• Tool enables silent malware installation via weaponized websites, bypassing all iPhone defenses
• L3Harris contractor Trenchant division developed the toolkit for US government surveillance operations
• Former L3Harris employees confirmed toolkit origins to TechCrunch on condition of anonymity
• Toolkit reportedly sold to Russian government by L3Harris employee, marking major intelligence breach
• Now being used by both state adversaries and cybercriminal groups beyond original US control
• Represents first confirmed case of US government hacking tools 'spinning out of control'
• Code analysis shows English-speaking developers and hallmarks matching previous US attribution

items.title: Iran Targets Amazon Data Centers in First Military Strike on Commercial Cloud Infrastructure
items.summary: Iran's Revolutionary Guard struck Amazon data centers in UAE and Bahrain, marking first military attack on commercial cloud infrastructure due to its dual military-civilian use supporting Pentagon operations.
items.content_text:
Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps launched drone strikes on Amazon data centers in UAE and Bahrain during U.S.-Israeli conflict
• First documented military attack specifically targeting private sector data centers in warfare
• Iran cited these facilities' role in 'supporting enemy military and intelligence activities' as justification
• Amazon, Google, Microsoft provide cloud services to Pentagon through programs like Joint Warfighter Cloud Capability
• Legal experts say dual-use cloud infrastructure becomes legitimate military target when supporting warfare
• Iranian media listed dozens of regional facilities including Google and Microsoft as 'Enemy Technology Infrastructure'
• Attacks caused structural damage, power disruption, and regional service outages across Middle East
• Blurred civilian-military boundaries in cloud computing complicate international law and targeting decisions
• Pentagon's AI integration under Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth expands potential military targets across U.S.
• Commercial cloud providers often cannot distinguish between civilian and military data processing

items.title: EFF Sues Medicare for Records on AI System Evaluating Patient Care Requests
items.summary: EFF filed FOIA lawsuit against CMS seeking transparency on WISeR program that uses AI to evaluate Medicare prior authorizations, affecting 6.4M seniors with potential bias and denial risks.
items.content_text:
EFF filed FOIA lawsuit against Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services over WISeR AI program
• WISeR uses AI algorithms to assess prior authorization requests for Medicare beneficiaries
• Program launched in January 2025 in six states, potentially affecting 6.4 million seniors
• Vendors compensated up to 20% of savings from denied healthcare services, creating perverse incentives
• No public information about AI training data, bias testing, or algorithmic safeguards
• Healthcare providers report delays in care approval and administrative strain since launch
• CMS has not responded to EFF's earlier FOIA request for vendor agreements and testing records
• Lawsuit seeks immediate processing of records about accuracy tests, bias audits, and vendor oversight

items.title: EFF Warns of Privacy Risks in Meta's Smart Glasses Surge
items.summary: EFF highlights serious privacy concerns as Meta's Ray-Ban smart glasses go mainstream, including human review of footage and covert recording capabilities that threaten bystander privacy.
items.content_text:
Meta's Ray-Ban smart glasses have moved from niche to mainstream adoption
• Footage automatically uploads to Meta AI app and undergoes human review for AI training
• Swedish investigation found Meta contractors reviewing sensitive footage including nudity
• Glasses designed to be indistinguishable from regular eyewear, making covert recording easy
• Small recording indicator light can be disabled with cheap modifications
• Constant recording creates new privacy risks like capturing passwords and bank details
• Google partnering with Warby Parker, Apple rumored to enter market
• Bystanders have limited recourse when recorded without consent

items.title: Court Blocks Pentagon Retaliation Against AI Company Refusing Surveillance Role
items.summary: Federal court granted preliminary injunction protecting Anthropic from Pentagon retaliation after company refused to remove AI guardrails for government surveillance operations.
items.content_text:
Northern District of California ruled Pentagon's 'supply chain risk' designation of Anthropic was First Amendment retaliation
• Anthropic refused Pentagon request to modify AI systems by removing surveillance guardrails
• Court found government actions were punitive rather than legitimate national security measures
• Pentagon reportedly wanted AI capabilities to analyze vast personal data troves including location, social media, and browsing data
• EFF and civil liberties groups filed amicus brief supporting Anthropic's constitutional challenge
• Case establishes precedent that companies cannot be compelled to rewrite code for government surveillance
• Highlights AI's capability to deanonymize speech and construct comprehensive personal profiles from aggregated data
• Decision reinforces that private sector guardrails are necessary absent congressional privacy protections

items.title: Ugandan Journalist Disappeared After Discussing Censorship Law Ruling
items.summary: Ugandan security forces forcibly disappeared journalist Mukose Arnold Anthony after he discussed a court ruling that struck down sections of the Computer Misuse Act used to target journalists and activists.
items.content_text:
Mukose Arnold Anthony, program director of Alternative Digitalk, was forcibly taken by plainclothes officers outside Metro FM 90.8 on March 22
• Disappearance occurred after live broadcast discussing Constitutional Court ruling that nullified sections of Computer Misuse Act
• Joint Anti-Terrorism Task Force operatives searched his home and seized electronic devices including laptop, modem, and smart watches
• Police confirmed they are holding Mukose in custody, investigation ongoing
• Pattern matches Uganda's documented enforced disappearance tactics, including high-profile cases like opposition bodyguard Eddie Mutwe
• Alternative Digitalk was previously raided in 2022 under Computer Misuse Act provisions later annulled by court
• Case demonstrates ongoing repression despite legal victories against censorship laws

items.title: MENA Region Hit by 52 Internet Shutdowns Across 15 Countries in 2025
items.summary: Access Now reports 313 global internet shutdowns in 2025, with MENA experiencing 52 shutdowns across 15 countries. Authorities increasingly target satellite internet alternatives.
items.content_text:
At least 313 internet shutdowns occurred globally in 52 countries during 2025, with not a single day without at least one shutdown
• MENA region experienced 52 shutdowns across 15 countries, with Iran leading at 11 shutdowns
• Armed conflict drives shutdowns in Palestine (7), Sudan (3), Syria (3), and Yemen (3), with Israel imposing all Palestinian shutdowns targeting Gaza
• Educational disruption systematic: Iraq imposed 6 exam-related shutdowns, while Algeria and Jordan blocked messaging apps during exams
• Authorities increasingly target Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite internet services in Iran, Syria, and Yemen as alternatives emerge
• Syria maintained Assad-era shutdown tactics despite regime change, demonstrating institutional continuity of digital repression
• Access Now calls for investment in 'secure, reliable, and scalable solutions that enable people to bypass shutdowns and censorship'

items.title: Global Internet Shutdowns Hit Record High: 313 Blackouts Across 52 Countries in 2025
items.summary: Access Now reports 2025 saw 313 internet shutdowns across 52 countries—a record high with no shutdown-free days. Myanmar led with 95 shutdowns, while authorities increasingly target satellite internet systems.
items.content_text:
313 internet shutdowns documented across 52 countries in 2025, up from 304 in 2024 [Access Now]
• Myanmar recorded highest number with 95 shutdowns for second consecutive year
• Seven first-time offenders including Albania, Cambodia, and the United States
• Conflict was primary trigger for 125 shutdowns (40% of total) across 14 countries
• 70 shutdowns coincided with grave human rights abuses including torture and war crimes in 21 countries
• Sharp increase in satellite internet targeting: 14 LEO satellite shutdowns vs 4 in 2024
• Satellite shutdowns occurred in Chad, Equatorial Guinea, Iran, Myanmar, Syria, Tanzania, Yemen
• 100 countries worldwide have now experienced shutdowns since tracking began in 2016
• Bangladesh took steps toward outlawing shutdowns; International Criminal Court recognized shutdown-crime link

items.title: EFF Warns of Privacy Risks in Mainstream Smartglasses Adoption
items.summary: EFF highlights privacy concerns with Meta Ray-Bans and other mainstream smartglasses, warning that third parties may access recorded content beyond just device owners.
items.content_text:
Smartglasses with embedded cameras and microphones have reached mainstream adoption
• EFF warns that Meta Ray-Bans raise significant privacy concerns for users and bystanders
• Third parties can potentially access recorded content, not just device owners
• EFF recommends users 'think twice' before purchasing or using Meta's Ray-Bans
• Privacy implications extend beyond individual users to broader surveillance concerns
• EFF covering this in newsletter alongside Internet Archive blocking and new leadership
• Represents shift toward ubiquitous recording devices in public spaces

items.title: EFF Documents Evolution of Digital Censorship Since Arab Spring
items.summary: EFF analysis shows how governments transformed from crude internet blocks to sophisticated censorship systems targeting platforms directly, making simple circumvention tools less effective.
items.content_text:
EFF published second part of series examining digital legacy of 2011 Arab uprisings
• 66% of internet users now live where political/social sites are blocked according to Freedom House
• 78% are in countries where people have been arrested for online posts
• Pre-2011: Governments used crude blocks that VPNs could easily bypass
• Post-2011: Governments shifted to pressuring platforms into compliance and sophisticated control systems
• Egypt exemplifies regression - went from Facebook-sparked revolution to comprehensive digital repression
• Egypt's 2018 Cybercrime Law criminalizes accessing banned sites (up to 6 months imprisonment)
• Vague 'national security' and 'public morals' provisions used to prosecute social media users
• Pattern replicated across Russia, Nigeria, Turkey with platform-targeted regulations

items.title: ICE Agents Now Checking IDs at TSA Checkpoints, Targeting Latinos
items.summary: Trump deployed ICE agents to TSA checkpoints at US airports, where they're requesting additional ID from Latino passengers, potentially as a 'test run' for expanded surveillance operations.
items.content_text:
ICE agents have replaced TSA staff at JFK Airport security checkpoints for ID verification
• Agents selectively requesting second form of ID from Latino passengers while letting white passengers pass normally
• Steve Bannon suggested on his podcast this may be a 'test run' ahead of November midterms [The Intercept]
• TSA agents training ICE personnel on checkpoint procedures while working unpaid [The Intercept]
• First-hand account documents differential treatment based on apparent ethnicity/surname
• Pattern suggests normalization strategy for expanded federal agent presence in daily life
• Deployment represents escalation from immigration enforcement to broader population control

items.title: EFF's Cindy Cohn Promotes Privacy Book at DC Events
items.summary: EFF Executive Director Cindy Cohn is hosting two DC events to discuss her new book about 30 years of fighting digital surveillance, with proceeds benefiting EFF.
items.content_text:
EFF Executive Director Cindy Cohn promoting book 'Privacy's Defender: My Thirty-Year Fight Against Digital Surveillance'
• Two Washington DC events scheduled for April 13-14, 2026
• April 13 event features conversation with AAPB Executive Director Gigi Sohn at Busboys & Poets
• April 14 event hosted with Women in Security and Privacy (WISP) featuring American University's Chelsea Horne
• Book endorsed by Edward Snowden as 'compelling account' and 'inspiring call to action'
